content.full_text:
LAW OFFICES

FREILICH, STONE, LEITNER & CARLISLE
KATHERINE E. STONE, P.C.
DEBORAH KAUFMAN
DEBORAH J. FOX
MARGARET MOORE SOHAGI

PHILIP A. SEYMOUR

WARREN J. ABBOTT
OF COUNSEL

A PARTNERSHIP INCLUDING PROFESSIONAL CORPORATIONS

The Wilshire Landmark, Suite 1230
11755 Wilshire Boulevard
Los Angeles, California 90025-1518

FACSIMILE
(310) 477-7663

TELEPHONE
(310) 444-7805

IN MISSOURI
FREILICH, LEITNER, CARLISLE & SHORTLIDGE
BOB PARKS WEST, 4800 MADISON
KANSAS CITY, MISSOURI 64112-3012
TELEPHONE (816) 561-4441

ROBERT H. FREILICH, P.C.*
NORMAN SPRINGER*
RICHARD G. CARLISLE, P.C.*
NEIL R. SHORTLIDGE, P.C.*
STEPHEN J. MOORE, P.C.*
STEPHEN P. CHINN, P.C.*
TERRY D. BURGESS*

PETER A. SCHLESINGER*,**
SUSAN P. SCHOETTLE*,*
S. MARK WHITE*,*
JOHN E. Rчелл*,*
ELIZABETH A. GARVIN*

DANIEL E. BULERAN
OF COUNSEL
ADMITTED IN MD* DE* NY* CO* GA*
TA* OR* NC* KS* HA* FL**

December 23, 1991

VIA FEDERAL EXPRESS

Blanca Breeze
Counsel, Legal Division
California Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street
Suite 800
Sacramento, California 95804-0807

Re: City of Riverside General Plan Update

CONFIDENTIAL:
ATTORNEY-CLIENT PRIVILEGE

Dear Ms. Breeze:

This office represents the City of Riverside, Mayor Teresa R. Frizzel
and Councilmember Robert A. Buster. This letter is for the purpose of seeking
clarification of your letter dated October 2, 1991 regarding Councilmember Buster
(your file no. I-91-266) and your letter dated March 19, 1991 regarding Mayor
Frizzel (your file no. A-90-768) as each pertain to the City's current general plan
revision. You have indicated that you did not have enough facts to determine if the
"public generally" exception may be applicable to decisions regarding the
general plan revision.

We have reviewed the statutes, regulations, opinions and advice letters
you provided to us, but none seem directly on point. A general plan is a long
term (usually twenty (20) years) comprehensive plan to guide the City's future
development. It consists of goals, policies and objectives and includes a diagram
designating general land uses. Zoning laws, subdivision regulation and other
detailed planning tools are subordinate to the general plan and provide the detail
necessary to guide development of individual parcels.

FREILICH, STONE, LEITNER & CARLISLE


Blanca Breeze
December 23, 1991
Page 2


        Because Riverside is a charter city, its zoning does not have to be
consistent with its general plan. (Government Code § 65860; Garat v. City of
Riverside, Case No. E007409/E007969 (tentative opinion); Verdugo Woodland
Homeowners, etc. Assn. v. City of Glendale (1986) 179 Cal. 3d 696.) Certain
areas of the City were re-zoned by initiative in 1979 (Measure R.) The zoning
cannot be changed except by vote of the people. The general plan revisions will
not affect the zoning. A subsequent initiative measure, Measure C, adopted in
1987 amended the general plan. The general plan revisions presently in progress
may not change the amendments that were adopted by initiative.

        We note that regulation 18702. l(b)(3) only deals with decisions
involving a "license, permit, or other entitlements to, or contract with, the subject
person or business." A general plan amendment is none of the above.

        Similarly, regulation 18702.1(a)(3)(b) only applies to decisions
involving "license, permit or other land use entitlement." A general plan revision
is not a "land use entitlement." (Selby Realty Co. v. City of San Buenaventura
(1973) 10 Cal.3d 110, 109 Cal.Rptr. 799, 514 P.2d 111.) "Zoning or rezoning,
annexation or deannexation, sale, purchase, or lease or inclusion in or exclusion
from . . . any district or other local subdivision, or real property" are mentioned
in Regulation 18702.1(a)(3)(A), but specifically excluded are the "amendment of
an existing zoning ordinance or other land use regulation which is applicable to all
other properties designated in that category." (Regulation 18702.1(a)(3)(E).)

        Although the councilmember's and the Mayor's properties will be
affected by the general plan revision, so will all the properties in the City
including those of all the councilmembers and staff who live in the City. None of
the criteria listed in 18702.1(a)(3) are met as to the general plan revision with
respect to either Councilman Buster or Mayor Frizzel's property. Their properties
will be affected the same as all other properties in the particular land use category
where they are located.

FREILICH, STONE, LEITNER & CARLISLE


Blanca Breeze
December 23, 1991
Page 3


     For example, Councilman Buster owns five (5) acres in the greenbelt
and receives income from the Royal Citrus Company. However, all the properties
in the greenbelt were zoned five (5) acre minimum by Measure R, the citizens'
initiative adopted in and by the voters in 1979. The general plan revisions will not
affect this zoning.

     Mayor Frizzel owns a home in an existing subdivision. The draft
general plan designates a land use category for this area that is compatible with the
existing use.

     It does not appear that the effect of a decision to adopt a general plan
revision is material under 18702.1(a)(3). General plan revisions are not mentioned
in the section and the exclusion of "an amendment of an existing zoning ordinance
or other land use regulations . . . which is applicable to all other properties
designated in that category." Regulation 18072.1(a)(3)(E) would seem to apply
with even more force to a general plan amendment which is more remote from any
kind of land use entitlement than zoning. "The general plan is atop the hierarchy
of local government law regulation land use . . . subordinate to the general plan
are zoning laws, which regulate the geographic allocation and allowed uses of
land." (Longtin, California Land Use, 2nd edition, p. 173.)

     According to subsection (b) of 18702.1 neither the Mayor nor the
Councilman would appear to be directly involved in the decision to revise the
general plan. This subsection only refers to decisions involving a "license, permit,
or other entitlement to, or contract with, the subject person or business entity".
(18702.1(b)(3).) A general plan revision is none of those things.

     In reviewing the regulation for determining whether an ownership
interest in real property is indirectly involved in a decision that will have a material
financial effect, (Regulation 18702.3), we note that the regulation only appears to
apply to decisions involving "construction of, or improvements to, streets, water,
sewer, storm drainage or similar facilities, and the real property in which the
official has an interest will receive new or substantially improved services." (18702.3(a)(3).)

FREILICH, STONE, LEITNER & CARLISLE


Blanca Breeze
December 23, 1991
Page 4


A general plan revision of course does not involve the construction of
or improvements to anything. (Selby Realty, 10 Cal.3d 110.) Decisions to
actually construct or improve facilities come much later, if at all.

Thus it does not appear that the mere ownership of real property or a
business in the City is the sort of economic interest that would disqualify the
Mayor or councilmembers from participating in the general plan revision, which
revision covers the entire City. This only makes sense as all councilmembers own
property and many are employees in the city.

In your letter of March 19, 1991, you indicated that Mayor Frizzel
might be disqualified from participating in a specific plan for over 700 acres of
property which is the subject of a specific plan. This specific plan is required of
Measure C which is itself a general plan amendment. This specific plan must be
consistent with Measures R and C. Mayor Frizzel's home is within thirty (30) feet
of the border of the specific plan area.

In Riverside, specific plans are part of the general plan. It seems to
us that the rational discussed above would apply equally to the specific plan and
that Mayor Frizzel should not be disqualified from participating in discussions
involving the specific plan.

We would very much appreciate your considering these ideas. John
Larson and I would like to meet with you soon to discuss this further.

Very truly yours,

KATHERINE E. STONE
of FREILICH, STONE, LEITNER & CARLISLE

cc:   John Woodhead, City Attorney
      Ernie Silva, League of California Cities

KES\Breeze3.ltr
70102-020

FREILICH, STONE, LEITNER & CARLISLE

Blanca Breeze
December 23, 1991
Page 5




bcc: Mayor Frizzel
     Councilman Buster
     John Larson

sections.question_synthetic: The counsel for the City of Riverside and its Mayor and Councilmember seeks clarification on whether the 'public generally' exception applies to decisions regarding the City's general plan revision, and whether the Mayor and Councilmember are disqualified from participating due to conflicts of interest in their personal real property holdings.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an incoming request/correspondence letter from a law firm to the FPPC, not an FPPC response. It references prior FPPC letters (I-91-266 and A-90768) but the current document is the incoming inquiry seeking clarification. No formal QUESTION/CONCLUSION sections present; information extracted from body of letter. References to specific statutes and regulations (Gov. Code 65860, FPPC Regulations 18702.1, 18702.3) provide context for the legal analysis being requested.

embedding.summary: This is an incoming legal request letter from counsel representing the City of Riverside, its Mayor Teresa R. Frizzel, and Councilmember Robert A. Buster, seeking FPPC clarification on whether they are disqualified from participating in the City's general plan update due to potential conflicts of interest involving their personal property holdings. The letter argues that general plan revisions affect all properties equally and should fall within the 'public generally' exception to conflict of interest rules.
